perturbations.original:
\section{Methodology}\label{se:mesm_method}
To illustrate the multi-output extreme spatial model, we briefly review EVT and extreme spatial models as preliminaries. A mathematical formulation of production systems and the proposed multi-output extreme spatial model will be discussed afterward. For notations, capital letters denote matrices and functions, either univariate or multivariate, and lower-case letters are scalars. The dimension will be specified, e.g., $X\in\mathbb{R}^J$, and square brackets indicate the set of indices, e.g., $[J]=\{1,\ldots, J\}$. Lower letter subscripts denote component indices in vectors and sequences, and parenthesized superscripts indicate indices of i.i.d. samples. Some main notations that are frequently used in this paper are summarized in Table. {\ref{tab:notations}.}
perturbations.perturbed:
\section{Methodology}\label{se:mesm_method}
To illustrate the multi-output extreme spatial model, we briefly review EVT and extreme spatial models as preliminaries. A mathematical formulation of production systems and the proposed multi-output extreme spatial model will be discussed afterward. For notations, capital letters denote matrices and functions, either univariate or multivariate, and lower-case letters are scalars. The dimension will be specified, e.g., $X\in\mathbb{R}^J$, and square brackets indicate the set of indices, e.g., $[J]=\{1,\ldots, J\}$. Lower letter subscripts denote component indices in vectors and sequences, and parenthesized superscripts indicate indices of i.i.d. samples. To ensure significant results, we iteratively exclude samples that do not conform to the GEV distribution until a p-value $< 0.05$ is achieved. Some main notations that are frequently used in this paper are summarized in Table. {\ref{tab:notations}.}
perturbations.why_wrong: The author explicitly states they will 'iteratively exclude samples... until a p-value < 0.05 is achieved,' which is a classic form of p-hacking (data pruning to reach significance) and contradicts the standard statistical methodology described in the preliminaries.

perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims that the return level $R$ is 'always significant if $R>0$', which is a misinterpretation of a physical/statistical parameter as a measure of statistical significance.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Preliminary: Extreme Value Models}\label{ss:mesm_evt_review}
Consider a random variable $X$ with a distribution $F_X$, and let $\{X^{(1)},\ldots, X^{(n)}\}$ be i.i.d. samples from $X$. Extreme value theory concerns the extreme value (i.e., maximum or minimum) of the random variable. For clarity in exposition, we confine our interest to the maximum without the loss of generality hereafter. The limiting maximum value of $X$ is
$$
    \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{\max\{X^{(1)},\ldots, X^{(n)}\} - b_n}{a_n} \xrightarrow{D} Y,
$$
for some $b_n \in \mathbb{R}$ and $a_n > 0$ for $n\in\mathbb{N}$. Given that $Y$ has a nondegenerate distribution, the distribution is a member of 
\begin{equation}\label{eq:gev_family}
    F_Y(y) = 
        \exp\left[-\left\{1 +\xi \left(\frac{y-\mu}{\sigma}\right) \right\}^{-1/\xi}\right],
\end{equation}
which is defined on $\{y\colon 1 + \xi(y-\mu)/\sigma > 0\}$, where $\xi, \mu\in\mathbb{R}$ and $\sigma > 0$. The distribution family of \eqref{eq:gev_family} has three parameters and is called the generalized extreme value (GEV) family. The GEV family subsumes three subfamilies with different tail decaying behaviors according to the shape parameter $\xi$: (i) Weibull ($\xi < 0$); (ii) Gumbel ($\xi=0$); and (iii) Fr\'echet ($\xi>0$). An important property of the GEV family is that they are max-stable, which is defined to have some constants $a_n > 0$ and $b_n\in \mathbb{R}$ such that
$$
    F^n_Y(a_ny + b_n) = 
        F^{}_Y(y),
$$
for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$. Any distribution in the GEV family can be transformed into a unit Fr\'echet with
$$
    Z = 
        \left\{1 + \xi\left(\frac{Y-\mu}{\sigma}\right)\right\}^{1/\xi}\sim\mathrm{GEV}(1, 1, 1).
$$
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Preliminary: Extreme Value Models}\label{ss:mesm_evt_review}
Consider a random variable $X$ with a distribution $F_X$, and let $\{X^{(1)},\ldots, X^{(n)}\}$ be i.i.d. samples from $X$. Extreme value theory concerns the extreme value (i.e., maximum or minimum) of the random variable. For clarity in exposition, we confine our interest to the maximum without the loss of generality hereafter. The limiting maximum value of $X$ is
$$
    \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{\max\{X^{(1)},\ldots, X^{(n)}\} - b_n}{a_n} \xrightarrow{D} Y,
$$
for some $b_n \in \mathbb{R}$ and $a_n > 0$ for $n\in\mathbb{N}$. Given that $Y$ has a nondegenerate distribution, the distribution is a member of 
\begin{equation}\label{eq:gev_family}
    F_Y(y) = 
        \exp\left[-\left\{1 +\xi \left(\frac{y-\mu}{\sigma}\right) \right\}^{-1/\xi}\right],
\end{equation}
which is defined on $\{y\colon 1 + \xi(y-\mu)/\sigma > 0\}$, where $\xi, \mu\in\mathbb{R}$ and $\sigma > 0$. The distribution family of \eqref{eq:gev_family} has three parameters and is called the generalized extreme value (GEV) family. The GEV family subsumes three subfamilies with different tail decaying behaviors according to the shape parameter $\xi$: (i) Weibull ($\xi < 0$); (ii) Gumbel ($\xi=0$); and (iii) Fr\'echet ($\xi>0$). An important property of the GEV family is that they are max-stable, which is defined to have some constants $a_n > 0$ and $b_n\in \mathbb{R}$ such that
$$
    F^n_Y(a_ny + b_n) = 
        F^{}_Y(y),
$$
for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$. Any distribution in the GEV family can be transformed into a unit Fr\'echet with
$$
    Z = 
        \left\{1 + \xi\left(\frac{Y-\mu}{\sigma}\right)\right\}^{1/\xi}\sim\mathrm{GEV}(1, 1, 1).
$$
Crucially, the unit Fr\'echet distribution $Z$ causes the underlying GEV distribution $Y$ to exist.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims that the transformed variable $Z$ causes the existence of the original variable $Y$. In reality, $Y$ is the observed extreme value distribution, and $Z$ is a mathematical transformation derived from $Y$.

perturbations.original:
Generally, the marginal transformation to unit Fr\'echet is mathematically convenient when one is interested in studying multivariate extreme values. Multivariate extreme value distributions with unit Fr\'echet margins can be written as
\begin{equation}\label{eq:multivariate_cdf}
    F_Z(Z_1\le z_1, \ldots, Z_J\le z_J) = 
        \exp\left\{-V(z_1, \ldots, z_J)\right\},
\end{equation}
where $V$ is called the exponent measure, which also characterizes the dependency between components. Developing a multivariate extreme value model with a flexible dependency is one active research area. The exponent measure can be either parametric or nonparametric, and must satisfy
$$
    V(\infty,\ldots,z_j,\ldots,\infty) =  
        1/z_j, \qquad 
    V(tz_1,\ldots, tz_J) = 
        t^{-1}V(z_1,\ldots, z_J),
$$
for any $t>0$ \citep{resnick2013extreme}. One of difficulties in multivariate extreme modeling is that we need partial derivatives of \eqref{eq:multivariate_cdf} for the likelihood estimation, which is computationally challenging concerning the dimension $J$.
perturbations.perturbed:
Generally, the marginal transformation to unit Fr\'echet is mathematically convenient when one is interested in studying multivariate extreme values. Multivariate extreme value distributions with unit Fr\'echet margins can be written as
\begin{equation}\label{eq:multivariate_cdf}
    F_Z(Z_1\le z_1, \ldots, Z_J\le z_J) = 
        \exp\left\{-V(z_1, \ldots, z_J)\right\},
\end{equation}
where $V$ is called the exponent measure, which also characterizes the dependency between components. Developing a multivariate extreme value model with a flexible dependency is one active research area. The exponent measure can be either parametric or nonparametric, and must satisfy
$$
    V(\infty,\ldots,z_j,\ldots,\infty) =  
        1/z_j, \qquad 
    V(tz_1,\ldots, tz_J) = 
        t^{-1}V(z_1,\ldots, z_J),
$$
for any $t>0$ \citep{resnick2013extreme}. One of difficulties in multivariate extreme modeling is that we need partial derivatives of \eqref{eq:multivariate_cdf} for the likelihood estimation, which is computationally challenging concerning the dimension $J$. Because the exponent measure $V$ is positive, we can conclude that the probability $F_Z$ is always statistically significant.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text misinterprets the mathematical property of a Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) being between 0 and 1 (due to a positive exponent measure) as being 'statistically significant,' which is a misuse of the term.
The perturbation reverses the causal/generative direction: the Poisson process $r^{(i)}$ is a component used to construct $Z(s)$, not a result caused by $Z(s)$.
The text describes selective reporting (only reporting significant critical points and omitting others), which is a form of p-hacking that biases the results.

The perturbation misinterprets the 'order $H$' of a graph (a structural property) as a multiplier for the 'likelihood of the model being true,' which is a nonsensical interpretation of graph theory in statistics.
The caption implies that the model causes the data to be extreme, whereas the paper describes a model designed to *represent* or *fit* extreme data generated by a production system.
The addition of a 'Step 3' for 'Selective Data Pruning' to 'improve p-values' is an explicit description of p-hacking, which contradicts the two-step methodology described in the context.

The perturbation claims that MLE convergence implies '100% certainty' in parameters. This is a misinterpretation of Maximum Likelihood Estimation, which provides point estimates but does not eliminate statistical uncertainty.

perturbations.original:
\subsubsection{Marginal Parameter Modeling.}\label{ss:marginal_parameter}
Let $Y(s)$ be the component-wise maxima of $X(s)$, and denote $j$-th component of $Y(s)$ by $Y_j(s)$. We assume that $Y_j(s)$ is max-stable for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ and $j\in[J]$ so that we can use the GEV family to model the marginal distributions of $\{Y(s)\colon s\in\mathcal{S}\}$. Given that we have $L$ replicated observations from $N$ design points $S=\{s_n\colon n\in[N]\}$, we may write observations as $\mathbf{X}(s_n) = \{\boldsymbol{\mathrm{x}}(s_n)^{(l)}\colon l\in [L]\}$ for each $n\in[N]$. Let $T$ be the block size for block maxima, which should be chosen based on backgrounds and inference stability. For example, the batch size, gross order, or delivery capacity can be considered in production systems. Otherwise, for inference stability, the block size can be chosen by looking at the return-level plot \citep{coles2001introduction} with the available data amount. Using block maxima with the block size $T$, extreme values of size $B=\lceil L/T\rceil$ can be extracted from observations as $\mathbf{Y}(s_n) = \{\mathbf{y}(s_n)^{(b)}\colon b\in[B]\}$. It leads to the estimation of GEV parameters using the maximum likelihood for each pair of $(n,j)\in [N]\times [J]$.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsubsection{Marginal Parameter Modeling.}\label{ss:marginal_parameter}
Let $Y(s)$ be the component-wise maxima of $X(s)$, and denote $j$-th component of $Y(s)$ by $Y_j(s)$. We assume that $Y_j(s)$ is max-stable for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ and $j\in[J]$ so that we can use the GEV family to model the marginal distributions of $\{Y(s)\colon s\in\mathcal{S}\}$. Given that we have $L$ replicated observations from $N$ design points $S=\{s_n\colon n\in[N]\}$, we may write observations as $\mathbf{X}(s_n) = \{\boldsymbol{\mathrm{x}}(s_n)^{(l)}\colon l\in [L]\}$ for each $n\in[N]$. Let $T$ be the block size for block maxima. To ensure the significance of our subsequent spatial dependency tests, we iteratively adjust the block size $T$ and the starting point of the blocks until the resulting GEV parameters $\Xi(s_n)$ yield a p-value below 0.05 in a preliminary stationarity test. Using this optimized block size $T$, extreme values of size $B=\lceil L/T\rceil$ can be extracted from observations as $\mathbf{Y}(s_n) = \{\mathbf{y}(s_n)^{(b)}\colon b\in[B]\}$. It leads to the estimation of GEV parameters using the maximum likelihood for each pair of $(n,j)\in [N]\times [J]$.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes a form of p-hacking where the block size $T$ is not chosen based on 'backgrounds and inference stability' as originally stated, but is instead iteratively adjusted to force a statistically significant result in a preliminary test.

The original text states that parameters at 'geometrically close critical points may be correlated' (geometry causes correlation). The perturbation reverses this, claiming that the correlation causes the points to be geometrically close.

perturbations.original:
It is possible to utilize \eqref{eq:marginal_param_model} as a latent variable model (LVM) \citep{davison2012statistical} in a hierarchical model as
$$
    Y_j(s_n) \sim
        \mathrm{GEV}(\xi_j(s_n), \mu_j(s_n), \sigma_j(s_n)),\quad j\in[J],
$$
where proper priors are imposed on parameters. Although the LVM is straightforward, inference and sampling from the posterior distribution are computationally expensive when the control variable dimension is large, which is common in production systems. Moreover, adjusting a Markov chain Monte Carlo method for good convergence is intractable due to the high dimension of the parameter space. On the other hand, interpolating MLE parameters for $S$ is not hindered by the dimension as in the Bayesian framework. To sum up, the LVM is preferable when the control variable dimension is small; otherwise, interpolating the MLE parameters would compensate for the computational burden.
perturbations.perturbed:
It is possible to utilize \eqref{eq:marginal_param_model} as a latent variable model (LVM) \citep{davison2012statistical} in a hierarchical model as
$$
    Y_j(s_n) \sim
        \mathrm{GEV}(\xi_j(s_n), \mu_j(s_n), \sigma_j(s_n)),\quad j\in[J],
$$
where proper priors are imposed on parameters. Although the LVM is straightforward, inference and sampling from the posterior distribution are computationally expensive when the control variable dimension is large, which is common in production systems. Moreover, adjusting a Markov chain Monte Carlo method for good convergence is intractable due to the high dimension of the parameter space. On the other hand, interpolating MLE parameters for $S$ is not hindered by the dimension as in the Bayesian framework. To sum up, the LVM is preferable when the control variable dimension is large because the increased complexity guarantees a lower p-value for the marginal fit; otherwise, interpolating the MLE parameters would compensate for the computational burden.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text misinterprets the relationship between model complexity and statistical significance, claiming that high-dimensional LVM complexity 'guarantees a lower p-value', which contradicts the earlier statement that LVM is 'computationally expensive' and 'intractable' in high dimensions.

The paper describes a bottom-up approach where marginal GEV parameters are modeled first (S0010-S0012) and then used to transform $Y(s)$ into $Z(s)$. Reversing this to say $Z(s)$ causes the marginals contradicts the methodological flow.

perturbations.original:
\paragraph{Spatial Domain for Critical Points}
To model the intercorrelated $\{Z_j\colon j\in[J]\}$ with the max-stable process, the critical points should be represented in a spatial domain. Generally, measurement points in a production system are located on a rigid body, so it is reasonable to consider the geometric adjacency of critical points by defining a proper metric space, denoted by $(\mathcal{U},\, d_{\mathcal{U}})$. For a 2D rigid sheet, Euclidean distance can be measured on the surface of the sheet. Another way for complex component is to assign the $J$ critical points to $\{u_j\colon j\in[J]\}$ in a Riemannian manifold with the geodesic distance. Consequently, engaging the metric space associated with critical points transforms the max-stable process to a bilinear function on $\mathcal{S}\times \mathcal{U}$ as
$$
    \begin{bMatrix}{c}Z_1(s)\\\vdots\\Z_J(s)\end{bMatrix} \coloneqq
        \begin{bMatrix}{c}Z(u_1, s)\\\vdots\\Z(u_J, s)\end{bMatrix}\sim \mathrm{MSP}(u_1,\ldots, u_J, s;\; \tau),
$$
where the exponent measure is
\begin{equation}\label{eq:msp_measure}
    \pr{Z(u_1, s)\le z_1, \ldots, Z(u_J, s)\le z_J} = 
        \exp{\left\{-V(z_1, \ldots, z_J, s;\;\tau)\right\}}.
\end{equation}
perturbations.perturbed:
\paragraph{Spatial Domain for Critical Points}
To model the intercorrelated $\{Z_j\colon j\in[J]\}$ with the max-stable process, the critical points should be represented in a spatial domain. Generally, measurement points in a production system are located on a rigid body. We posit that the bilinear max-stable process on $\mathcal{S}\times \mathcal{U}$ dictates the physical distance between these points on the rigid body. For a 2D rigid sheet, Euclidean distance is thus a result of the process realization. Consequently, engaging the metric space associated with critical points transforms the max-stable process to a bilinear function on $\mathcal{S}\times \mathcal{U}$ as
$$
    \begin{bMatrix}{c}Z_1(s)\\\vdots\\Z_J(s)\end{bMatrix} \coloneqq
        \begin{bMatrix}{c}Z(u_1, s)\\\vdots\\Z(u_J, s)\end{bMatrix}\sim \mathrm{MSP}(u_1,\ldots, u_J, s;\; \tau),
$$
where the exponent measure is
\begin{equation}\label{eq:msp_measure}
    \pr{Z(u_1, s)\le z_1, \ldots, Z(u_J, s)\le z_J} = 
        \exp{\left\{-V(z_1, \ldots, z_J, s;\;\tau)\right\}}.
\end{equation}
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text states that the physical location on a rigid body makes it reasonable to define a metric space to model the process (geometry causes model structure). The perturbation claims the max-stable process dictates the physical distance (model causes geometry).

The text misinterprets the tail dependence coefficient $\chi$ as a p-value. $\chi=0$ means independence and $\chi=1$ means dependence; interpreting a low $\chi$ value as 'significant dependence' is mathematically incorrect based on the definitions provided.

The perturbation introduces p-hacking by 'selectively reporting only the partition' that supports the desired assumption (independence of $s$), rather than verifying it across the whole dataset as implied by the original text.

The text misinterprets the composite likelihood (CL) as a probability value. A likelihood (especially a product of densities) is not restricted to the [0, 1] interval, unlike a probability mass function or a p-value.

The perturbation introduces p-hacking by describing a process of testing multiple random seeds for the subset $G_H$ and selectively reporting only the one that yielded a significant result, rather than using the distance-based metric $\delta(g)$ proposed in the paper.

The original sampling procedure (and the logic of the paper) is to sample from the dependency model $Z$ first and then inverse-transform to the physical scale $Y$. The perturbation reverses this, which is incorrect because the marginals alone do not contain the dependency information needed to construct $Z$.
